attributes.stem:
A 50-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with rapidly progressive weakness in his legs. Over the past two days, he developed progressive difficulty walking and is now unable to lift his legs off the bed. He has no pain in his lower limb otherwise. He has no arm weakness or dyspnoea. He had an episode of non-bloody diarrhoea a few weeks prior, which has since resolved. He has a history of hypertension and takes hydrochlorothiazide.

On examination, he has symmetrical lower limb weakness, absent reflexes and intact sensation. His laboratory results, including electrolytes, renal function, and blood counts, are normal. CT head shows no acute findings.

A lumbar puncture is performed, and analysis reveals 3 white blood cells/mm3 (normal range <5 cells/mm3), protein 1.0 g/L (normal range <0.5 g/L), and Gram stain negative.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
The patient is presenting with an ascending paralysis, absent reflexes and albuminocytologic dissociation, consistent with a diagnosis of Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), which is an autoimmune demyelination process of the peripheral nervous system. This is in the setting of a preceding diarrhoeal illness, a rare but serious post-infectious immune-mediated neuropathy.

Intravenous immunoglobulin is a treatment that involves infusing a solution containing antibodies from multiple donors into the patient's bloodstream. IVIG is one of the first-line treatments for GBS and has been shown to be effective in reducing inflammation and improving outcomes. The use of IVIG in GBS is supported by Australian guidelines and is the most appropriate treatment for the patient's current presentation. Another management strategy is the use of plasma exchange which can be considered within 4 weeks of initial symptoms.

Prednisone is a steroid medication that is often used to reduce inflammation in a variety of conditions. However, it is not the most appropriate treatment for the patient's current presentation. The patient's symptoms and examination findings are consistent with Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), an autoimmune disorder that affects the peripheral nervous system. The use of steroids in GBS is controversial and has not been shown to be effective in improving outcomes. In fact, some studies have suggested that the use of steroids may actually delay recovery and increase the risk of complications.

Ciprofloxacin is not the most appropriate treatment for this patient's current presentation. The patient's symptoms and examination findings are consistent with GBS, an autoimmune disorder that is not caused by a bacterial infection.

Pyridostigmine is a medication that is used to treat myasthenia gravis, a neuromuscular disorder that causes muscle weakness and fatigue. However, it is not the most appropriate treatment for the patient's current presentation. While some patients with GBS may experience muscle weakness and fatigue, pyridostigmine is not effective in treating the inflammation that is the underlying cause of GBS.

Botulism antitoxin is a medication that is used to treat botulism, a rare, but serious form of food poisoning caused by the bacterium Clostridium botulinum. It is not the most appropriate treatment for the patient's current presentation. It would be beneficial if the patient has symptoms suggestive of botulism; a descending paralysis.
learningPoints: The treatment of Guillain-Barré syndrome is either IVIG or plasma exchange as the first-line treatment.
Guillain-Barré syndrome often follows a preceding infection, such as a diarrhoeal illness, and presents with ascending paralysis and absent reflexes.
references: Nguyen TP, Taylor RS. Guillain-Barre Syndrome. 2023 Feb 7. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan–. PMID: 30335287. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30335287/

Leonhard SE, Mandarakas MR, Gondim FAA, Bateman K, Ferreira MLB, Cornblath DR, van Doorn PA, Dourado ME, Hughes RAC, Islam B, Kusunoki S, Pardo CA, Reisin R, Sejvar JJ, Shahrizaila N, Soares C, Umapathi T, Wang Y, Yiu EM, Willison HJ, Jacobs BC. Diagnosis and management of Guillain-Barré syndrome in ten steps. Nat Rev Neurol. 2019 Nov;15(11):671-683. doi: 10.1038/s41582-019-0250-9. Epub 2019 Sep 20. PMID: 31541214; PMCID: PMC6821638. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31541214/

Hughes RA, Swan AV, van Doorn PA. Intravenous immunoglobulin for Guillain-Barré syndrome. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014 Sep 19;2014(9):CD002063. doi: 10.1002/14651858.CD002063.pub6. PMID: 25238327; PMCID: PMC6781841. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25238327/
